# Aeroelasticity in Wind Turbines

![This close-up composition showcases robust industrial pipe connectors and components, emblematic of sustainable energy infrastructure. The image emphasizes material durability and potential for extended lifespan, key aspects of resource conservation in power generation systems. Dark pipes and a textured connector, likely constructed from durable materials, are set against a muted background, suggesting a focus on energy transmission or distribution networks within a modern grid.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-energy-system-infrastructure-components-close-up-on-industrial.jpg "Sustainable Energy System Infrastructure Components Close up on Industrial Piping and Connectors for Efficient Resource Use")

## Fundamentals

Aeroelasticity in [wind turbines Meaning → Wind turbines convert wind power into electricity for sustainable energy.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/term/wind-turbines/)  represents a fundamental interaction often simplified when initially considering these [renewable energy Meaning → Energy from sources that replenish naturally, offering a sustainable alternative to fossil fuels.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/term/renewable-energy/)  structures. At its most basic, its meaning refers to the interplay of aerodynamic forces, elastic forces, and inertial forces acting upon a flexible structure immersed in airflow. Consider a wind turbine blade → it is not a rigid object. Wind, an aerodynamic force, pushes against it.

The blade bends and twists, demonstrating elastic deformation. As it moves, it possesses mass and velocity, introducing inertial forces. Aeroelasticity studies how these three types of forces influence one another and, critically, how this dynamic interaction can lead to unstable structural behavior. Understanding this is vital for constructing turbines that operate safely and durably within variable atmospheric conditions, ensuring the longevity required for sustainable energy generation projects.

This field’s basic designation for wind turbines addresses potential issues like excessive vibrations, uncontrolled blade motion, or even structural failure. A stable system sees these forces balancing out or damping oscillations. Instability arises when energy from the airflow is transferred into structural vibrations faster than internal damping mechanisms can dissipate it, leading to growing oscillations. Think of pushing a child on a swing; small, timed pushes (like wind gusts) add energy, making the swing go higher (growing oscillation).

If the pushes are mistimed or too strong relative to the swing’s inherent resistance, the motion could become unpredictable or damaging. Wind turbines face similar complexities on a much grander scale, dealing with forces far exceeding human capability.

For someone new to this topic, the essence of aeroelasticity lies in acknowledging that [wind turbine blades Meaning → Wind turbine blades convert wind’s energy into electricity.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/term/wind-turbine-blades/)  are not static elements merely resisting wind; they are dynamic components that respond fluidly to their environment. This inherent flexibility, necessary for managing variable loads and reducing peak stresses, introduces challenges. The very act of bending alters the blade’s angle relative to the wind (aerodynamics), which in turn changes the aerodynamic forces, causing further bending (elastic), and affecting the blade’s motion (inertia).

This cyclical dependency is what aeroelasticity investigates. Its initial explication focuses on identifying critical wind speeds or operational conditions where this feedback loop might turn detrimental.

Aeroelasticity explores how aerodynamic wind forces, a blade’s elastic flexibility, and its inertial motion interact on wind turbines.

Investigating this interplay begins by looking at specific, simpler phenomena that combine these forces. One readily understood concept is that of flutter. While more complex in wind turbines than aircraft wings, at a foundational level, flutter involves coupled bending and twisting vibrations that can rapidly increase in amplitude. Imagine a flag flapping wildly in the wind → the wind forces cause it to bend and twist, and this changing shape alters how the wind acts upon it, continuing the motion.

For a turbine blade, flutter would represent uncontrolled, often destructive, oscillatory movement driven by wind speed. Another simple concept is divergence, a static instability where an increasing wind load causes deformation (like bending) that further increases the load, leading to runaway deformation without oscillation. While less common for typical turbine blades compared to flutter, it demonstrates the potential for structural failure originating from static aeroelastic interactions.

### Basic Components and Interactions

The fundamental components involved in turbine aeroelasticity are tangible and relatable.

The interaction among these elements creates the aeroelastic system. As the blade rotates and encounters wind, aerodynamic pressures generate lift and drag forces. These forces induce structural deformations → bending out of the plane of rotation (flapwise) and twisting about the blade’s axis (torsional). These deformations modify the blade’s shape and orientation relative to the wind, consequently altering the aerodynamic forces themselves.

Simultaneously, the motion associated with these deformations generates inertial forces. These forces are all dynamically coupled, forming a continuous feedback loop that aeroelastic analysis seeks to understand and predict.

Even seemingly simple aspects like blade weight distribution carry significance. Placing more weight towards the tip affects inertial forces, influencing vibrational frequencies and response characteristics. Similarly, altering the blade’s stiffness through design changes impacts its elastic response. A stiffer blade deforms less under load but might experience higher stresses.

A more flexible blade deforms more but can shed peak loads. The design involves a delicate balance, where aeroelastic considerations guide decisions about materials, geometry, and mass distribution. Early wind turbines, often simpler in design, still contended with these basic principles, though the scale and flexibility of modern multi-megawatt turbines amplify the complexities considerably, requiring sophisticated analysis from the outset of their specification.

#### Initial Challenges and Solutions

Early on, designers recognized the potential for wind-induced vibrations. Simple solutions included making blades very stiff or operating turbines only within narrow, predictable wind ranges. However, to generate substantial power efficiently, turbines needed larger, lighter, and more flexible blades capable of operating in a wider array of wind conditions.

This drive towards increased scale and efficiency intensified the need to understand and mitigate aeroelastic phenomena rigorously. The basic meaning evolved from merely acknowledging the interaction to quantifying and controlling it.

Basic [mitigation Meaning → Mitigation focuses on efforts to reduce or prevent the emission of greenhouse gases, tackling the root causes of climate change.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/)  strategies often focused on passive design features:

While these fundamental approaches form the bedrock of aeroelastic design, their effectiveness diminishes rapidly as turbine size increases and operational demands grow. The basic specification of a wind turbine blade today inherently includes aeroelastic stability requirements, alongside static strength and fatigue life, as non-negotiable design criteria. The initial definition might have been reactive, addressing problems encountered; today, the design process is proactive, predicting and preventing these interactions during the digital modeling phase, long before any physical prototype is built. This proactive stance is paramount for scaling [wind energy Meaning → Wind energy harnesses wind’s kinetic force to generate clean electricity, a vital component of global sustainable energy solutions.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/term/wind-energy/)  sustainably.

Basic Force Interactions

| Force Type | Source | Blade Response | Aeroelastic Role (Simplified) |
| Aerodynamic | Wind flow over airfoil | Lift, Drag, Pressure Distribution | Primary driver of load and instability |
| Elastic | Blade material stiffness | Bending, Twisting deformation | Resists deformation, provides restoring force |
| Inertial | Blade mass and motion | Resistance to acceleration | Influences vibration frequencies and dynamics |

Understanding aeroelasticity starts with appreciating this simple yet crucial force triangle. Ignoring any vertex leads to an incomplete specification of the turbine’s behavior. The interaction is continuous; wind changes, blade responds, response changes wind interaction, process repeats.

This cycle can either dampen itself out, resulting in stable operation, or amplify, causing significant issues. The simple description of aeroelasticity thus focuses on the potential for this dynamic interaction to lead to problems, setting the stage for more detailed analysis needed for modern large-scale machines essential for global energy transitions towards renewable sources.

In summary, for those beginning to understand wind turbine engineering, aeroelasticity is the field acknowledging that blades move and deform significantly under wind load, and this movement feeds back into how the wind acts on the blade. This simple meaning highlights why simply designing a blade strong enough to withstand static wind pressure is insufficient. Its inherent dynamic nature requires consideration of the interplay of forces to ensure safe, long-term operation, a fundamental aspect of making wind energy a reliable pillar of sustainable power infrastructure. This foundational understanding provides the basis for delving into more complex behaviors and analysis techniques.

## Intermediate

Transitioning from the fundamental concept, the intermediate meaning of aeroelasticity in wind turbines expands upon the simple interplay of forces to examine specific phenomena and their structural implications. Here, aeroelasticity signifies a complex dynamic coupling where deformations influence aerodynamic loads, and these altered loads drive further deformations. This intricate relationship can lead to self-excited oscillations.

Unlike forced vibrations caused by specific external frequencies (like tower shadow or rotational imbalances), self-excited oscillations draw energy directly from the airflow itself as a result of the motion, capable of occurring at characteristic frequencies dependent on the system’s aeroelastic properties. The critical aspect at this level involves understanding the mechanisms by which energy is transferred from the wind into the structure through this coupling.

One key intermediate concept is understanding how different modes of vibration couple together. A blade might have natural frequencies for bending and natural frequencies for twisting. Independently, these might be manageable. However, under aerodynamic load, bending can induce twisting, and twisting can induce bending.

If the frequencies and phasing of these motions align in a particular way, energy can be continuously extracted from the wind, leading to rapidly increasing oscillations. This coupled motion is central to classic flutter instability. The precise clarification of this coupling necessitates understanding the blade’s structural properties, its mass distribution, and the aerodynamic forces acting on it in a dynamic state.

Stall flutter represents another significant intermediate-level phenomenon. Unlike classical flutter which typically occurs when airfoils are operating within their linear, attached-flow range, stall flutter happens when sections of the blade operate in deep stall. In this regime, the airflow separates from the blade surface in a complex, non-linear manner. This separation is highly sensitive to angle of attack changes caused by blade motion.

As the blade twists or bends, the separation pattern shifts dramatically, causing rapid fluctuations in aerodynamic forces (especially pitching moments). These fluctuating forces, tied directly to the motion that caused them, can drive large amplitude oscillations, often torsional, which are difficult to predict using simpler linear models. The significance here lies in recognizing that aerodynamic behavior in stall is inherently non-linear and profoundly impacts dynamic stability.

Intermediate aeroelasticity involves understanding specific coupled vibrations like flutter and stall flutter, where structural motion directly feeds back into aerodynamic forces.

Analyzing these phenomena at an intermediate level requires moving beyond static or simple dynamic force balance. It necessitates considering the blade as a flexible beam with distributed mass and stiffness, subject to time-varying aerodynamic loads that depend on the blade’s instantaneous position, velocity, and angle of attack. This often involves employing simplified aeroelastic models.

These models typically discretize the blade into segments and apply simplified aerodynamic theories, such as Theodorsen’s unsteady airfoil theory for attached flow or empirical models for stall conditions, combined with structural beam models (like Bernoulli-Euler or Timoshenko beam theories) to represent the blade’s flexibility. This approach provides a workable framework for predicting the onset speed of instabilities under simplified conditions.

### Modeling Approaches and Parameters

Intermediate aeroelastic analysis often relies on identifying critical parameters that govern stability. The reduced velocity is one such parameter, relating the wind speed to the structural frequency and characteristic length (like blade chord). It provides an indication of how many structural oscillation cycles occur as the wind traverses a certain distance along the blade, influencing the timing of aerodynamic force variations relative to structural motion.

Another parameter is the mass ratio, comparing the mass of the blade to the mass of the surrounding air displaced by its motion. These non-dimensional parameters offer generalized ways to assess the susceptibility of a design to aeroelastic instability across different scales.

Modeling at this stage typically employs simplified aerodynamic representations. Unsteady aerodynamics, acknowledging that forces don’t react instantaneously to changes in blade motion or wind speed, becomes important. Models incorporate time lags or ‘wake dynamics’ to capture the memory effects of the airflow downstream of the blade.

Combining these with a modal representation of the blade’s structural dynamics → describing how the blade vibrates in terms of characteristic shapes or modes (like the first flapwise bending mode, first edgewise bending mode, first torsional mode) → allows analysts to study how these modes interact and potentially couple aeroelastically. The intermediate interpretation thus moves from generalized concepts to specific dynamic interactions and their prediction through parameterized models.

The specification of these design features requires iterative analysis. Initial designs are analyzed using intermediate models. If instabilities are predicted within the operational wind speed range, the design parameters are adjusted (e.g., increasing torsional stiffness, altering mass distribution) and re-analyzed. This process continues until the desired level of aeroelastic stability is achieved, typically ensuring that the flutter speed, for example, is significantly higher than the maximum operational wind speed the turbine is designed to withstand.

#### Operational Implications and Sensing

Aeroelastic effects are not just theoretical concerns; they directly impact turbine operation and longevity. Excessive aeroelastic vibrations can lead to accelerated fatigue damage in blades, hubs, and drivetrains. They can cause undesirable noise emissions and reduce power production by causing uncontrolled pitching or flapping motion.

Furthermore, large amplitude oscillations can lead to structural failure, posing significant safety risks and economic losses. The practical significance at the intermediate level includes assessing these operational consequences and incorporating means to mitigate them during operation, not just design.

Sensing and monitoring systems play a role in managing aeroelastic effects on operational turbines. While sophisticated active control based on detailed aeroelastic state estimation is more advanced, intermediate systems might use simple sensors to detect blade vibrations (e.g., accelerometers, strain gauges). Deviation from expected vibration levels or the onset of specific spectral peaks can serve as indicators of potential aeroelastic issues or structural health degradation.

This data provides valuable feedback, allowing operators to curtail turbine operation in challenging wind conditions or schedule inspections and maintenance proactively. The description of monitoring systems at this stage highlights their role in validating design predictions and informing operational strategies.

Intermediate Aeroelastic Phenomena & Characteristics

| Phenomenon | Mechanism | Key Coupling | Aerodynamic Regime | Primary Consequence |
| Classical Flutter | Coupled bending/torsion extracts energy from flow | Flapwise Bending – Torsion | Attached Flow (linear) | Rapidly growing oscillation, potential failure |
| Stall Flutter | Motion-dependent force fluctuations in stalled flow | Torsion (often) – Flapwise Bending | Separated Flow (non-linear) | Large amplitude, often torsional, oscillations |
| Gust Response | Turbulence exciting structural modes | All modes excited simultaneously | Transient, non-uniform flow | Increased dynamic loads, fatigue accumulation |

Understanding the response to turbulent wind is another aspect covered at this level. Turbulent gusts contain energy across a range of frequencies. When the frequencies present in turbulence coincide with the blade’s natural frequencies, even if these modes are not strongly coupled in a classical flutter sense, large forced vibrations can occur. Aeroelastic analysis helps predict the blade’s dynamic response to these stochastic (random) loads, allowing engineers to estimate the fatigue loads the blade will experience over its lifetime.

This moves the focus from just stability (onset of unbounded oscillations) to dynamic response amplitude under realistic, turbulent wind conditions. The implications for structural integrity and long-term fatigue life are central to sustainable operation.

In essence, the intermediate interpretation of [wind turbine aeroelasticity Meaning → Wind turbine aeroelasticity involves the coupled dynamics between wind forces and the turbine structure’s flexible response, crucial for stability and loads.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/term/wind-turbine-aeroelasticity/)  builds upon the foundational interaction by detailing specific instability mechanisms like flutter and stall flutter, introducing simplified modeling techniques, exploring critical non-dimensional parameters, and discussing the direct impact of these phenomena on turbine operation, durability, and the need for monitoring. This level of understanding provides engineers with the tools to analyze common aeroelastic problems and design blades that are stable and robust under typical operating conditions, moving closer to the rigorous analysis required for certification and advanced design practices. This deeper insight is fundamental to developing and deploying larger turbines essential for meeting ambitious renewable energy targets.

## Academic

The academic meaning of aeroelasticity in wind turbines transcends basic descriptions and intermediate analyses, constituting a highly specialized field demanding sophisticated theoretical frameworks and computational tools. It is the comprehensive, non-linear science investigating the reciprocal, dynamic interactions among distributed aerodynamic loads, elastic structural deformations, inertial responses, and control system actions within the operating envelope of a flexible rotor-nacelle-tower system, particularly focusing on predicting and preventing instabilities and excessive dynamic loads across a vast range of atmospheric and operational conditions. Its specification demands precise mathematical representation of complex physics, often pushing the boundaries of computational capabilities and theoretical modeling.

This level concerns not only the prevention of catastrophic instabilities like flutter but also the accurate prediction of dynamic load spectra that dictate fatigue life, acoustic emissions, power quality, and the overall performance and reliability of multi-megawatt turbines. The interpretation involves delving into advanced topics like geometrically exact beam theory, non-linear aerodynamic effects beyond simple stall, coupled system dynamics including drivetrain and tower, and the influence of active control systems on stability margins.

From an academic standpoint, the significance of aeroelasticity lies in enabling the design and certification of increasingly larger and more flexible wind turbines, which are critical for reducing the levelized cost of energy (LCOE) and scaling renewable power generation. Larger rotors capture more energy, but blade mass and cost scale roughly with the cube of the length, while loads scale less rapidly. This economic pressure drives designers towards more flexible structures to shed peak loads and reduce material use.

This push, however, inherently increases the potential for aeroelastic instabilities and large dynamic deflections. Therefore, academic research provides the foundational understanding and advanced computational methodologies necessary to confidently design these complex, cost-optimized, yet reliable structures, ensuring they meet stringent safety and performance standards over a projected 20-30 year operational life.

Academically, the exploration involves dissecting diverse perspectives on the problem. One perspective focuses on structural modeling → moving beyond simple beam models to [geometrically non-linear, rotating beam theories](https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/1249197) or even full three-dimensional finite element models for highly complex blade geometries or localized stress analyses. Another perspective centers on aerodynamic modeling → employing lifting line theories with sophisticated unsteady aerodynamic corrections, or higher-fidelity methods like prescribed wake models, Free Wake models, or full-blown computational fluid dynamics (CFD) coupled with structural solvers.

A third view considers the coupled system dynamics, integrating models of the flexible tower, drivetrain, and control system into the aeroelastic model. Each perspective offers different insights and levels of fidelity, with computational cost increasing significantly with complexity.

Academic aeroelasticity requires sophisticated modeling of complex physics, predicting dynamic loads and instabilities in integrated turbine systems under varied conditions.

A deep-level interpretation acknowledges that the traditional separation of dynamics, aerodynamics, and structures is artificial in wind turbines. Aeroelasticity sits precisely at their intersection. Its essence is the feedback loop → structural motion (dynamics/structures) changes local angle of attack and relative velocity (aerodynamics), altering forces/moments (aerodynamics) that induce further structural motion (structures/dynamics).

This definition extends to include interactions with the tower’s flexibility, which can couple with blade and nacelle motion (e.g., tower fore-aft or side-to-side modes coupling with blade flapwise or edgewise modes) and the drivetrain dynamics (e.g., torsional oscillations in the shaft coupling with blade torsional modes). Understanding these cross-system influences is paramount for a complete academic delineation.

Executive Summary Aeroelastic modeling (AM) is the primary methodology for the structural and performance assessment of any wind turbine and provides an understanding of the impact of design parameters on wind turbine loading and power response before witnessing it in the field. Despite these advantages, the use of AM in the distributed wind technology sector is limited, especially by the less established manufacturers. This project represents an in-depth assessment of the status of AM and its role within the standards for the distributed wind technology industry. The study gathered input and feedback from a large number of national and international stakeholders, reviewed technical strengths and weaknesses of the current edition of the design standards, analyzed the minutes from recent industry workshops and meetings, collected publicly available AM templates, and provided an evaluation of the existing AM codes. The study achieved several goals, including providing strategies for the loads assessment categorization of wind turbines based on rotor-swept area and archetype, and guidance for AM verification and validation (V&V), which includes discussions of measurement requirements and a sample test plan for future V&V campaigns and design standards development. This document summarizes the different tasks conducted in the course of the project and highlights the steps required to improve AM adoption based on a multifaceted approach that encompasses (1) augmenting AM software capabilities, (2) publishing AM best practices and design bases, (3) creating new model templates, (4) providing guidance for V&V of codes and specific wind turbine models leveraging field testing best practice, and (5) addressing weaknesses in the current standards. Many of the future objectives identified in this study could leverage the National Renewable Energy Laboratory’s upcoming testing campaigns of three modern distributed wind turbines. Recommendations within this study will advance the value and the ease-of-use of AM, thereby allowing the industry to better capitalize this underutilized tool, resulting in a more efficient design process, an easier path to certification, and overall better and more reliable distributed wind turbine products. vi This report is available at no cost from the National Renewable Energy Laboratory at www.nrel.gov/publications. Top-Priority Improvement Strategies for Future Standards Development (see Section 8 for more details).................................................................................................................................. 69 1 This report is available at no cost from the National Renewable Energy Laboratory at www.nrel.gov/publications. 1 Project Introduction From the request for proposal from the National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL; NREL 2021b): “A primary objective of the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) Wind Energy Technologies Office is to increase the number of certified small and midsize wind systems. Another DOE objective is to improve national and international technical standards that are used as the baseline for wind turbine certification. The U.S. distributed wind turbine industry is the global leader of installed capacity of distributed wind turbines but is a micro-capitalized industry that is under competitive pressure from non-U.S. manufacturers, some without certification. The Strategic and Technical Engagement effort has a specific focus to improve the certification process around small and midsized distributed wind technology. Of specific interest is understanding how better to utilize decades of distributed wind turbine modeling and measurement expertise to both improve and simplify the certification of small and midsized wind turbines.” The International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) 61400-2 standard is up for revision in 2022. The American Wind Energy Association (AWEA) small wind turbine (SWT) standard 9.1–2009 has been revised into an American National Standards Institute (ANSI) standard. The International Energy Agency Wind Technology Collaboration Programme (IEA Wind) Task 41 (IEA Wind 2020a) mentions that standards and certification are seen as obstacles to innovation and result in slow market penetration of small wind turbines. NREL contracted RRD Engineering, LLC, (RRD) for support and guidance on the use of aero-hydro-servo-elastic (AHSE) codes (e.g., OpenFAST) for the distributed wind technology (DWT) sector. In particular, RRD was tasked with identifying the strengths and weaknesses of current standards (e.g., IEC 61400-2:2013 [IEC 2013]) and developing a plan to exercise and validate specific wind turbine aeroelastic models. The goal is to help NREL strategize, develop, and implement a research method to improve national and international standards. RRD partnered with Windward Engineering, LC, to conduct this work and to leverage their knowledge of IEC 61400-2:2013 and other international standards, as well as their expertise in wind turbine testing and loads measurement to support an update of how aeroelastic models can improve the certification process. In Section 2, a summary is presented of recent stakeholder meetings together with key conclusions on the matters of simplified loads methodology (SLM), aeroelastic modeling (AM), and duration testing. Section 3 presents the recommended steps to substantiate a breakdown of wind turbine categories for loads assessment following AM and SLM in particular. Section 4 discusses the turbine-specific aeroelastic models that were retrieved in the public domain, their strengths and weaknesses, and suggested general actions to improve AM codes and their widespread adoption. In Section 5, the role of testing to support the development of AM code and design standards is described together with a review of the verification and validation (V&V) framework and an in-depth account of typical best practices in field testing and measurements that can further facilitate these activities. At the end of the section, a summary of recommended actions is offered to leverage upcoming wind turbine testing opportunities at NREL’s Flatirons Campus. Section 6 provides recommendations for the use and improvement of specific AM code capabilities and the use of the retrieved turbine AM templates. Technical 2 This report is available at no cost from the National Renewable Energy Laboratory at www.nrel.gov/publications. strengths and weaknesses of the current design standards (focus is on IEC 61400-2:2013) as they pertain to AM and as perceived after conducting expert elicitations and a dedicated workshop are discussed in Section 7. In the same section, we describe a wind turbine category breakdown matrix based on the input received at the workshop, which uses both turbine size and archetype characteristics as primary differentiating parameters, and which can be used to specify minimum requirements for loads assessment and validation of AM. Section 8 proposes strategies for design standard improvements based on the analysis conducted in this project and feedback from the industry stakeholders. Concluding remarks and tables of recommended future work activities to improve the adoption of AM in the distributed wind energy industry are presented in Section 9. 3 This report is available at no cost from the National Renewable Energy Laboratory at www.nrel.gov/publications. 2 Summary of Recent Discussions and Findings Among Stakeholders This section constitutes a brief memo that summarizes the weaknesses in design standards for DWT as identified by national and international meetings that occurred in 2019 and 2020 as indicated in (NREL 2021a). The meetings include: • Standards Forum held on February 27, 2019, (during the Distributed Wind Energy Association [DWEA] business conference) denoted as DWEA0227, with detailed notes provided by NREL in (Baring-Gould, Preus, and van Dam 2019) • ANSI/AWEA SWT-1 (to become ANSI/American Clean Power [ACP] 101-1) meetings collectively denoted as ACP2020: o In-person meeting held on February 13, 2020, in Denver, Colorado, with notes provided in (NREL 2020a) o In-person meeting held on February 28, 2020, in Arlington, Virginia, with notes in (NREL 2020b; Baring-Gould 2020; Forsyth 2020) o Virtual meeting held on April 9, 2020, with meeting minutes in (NREL 2020c) • European International Standards Assessment Forum held June 26–28, 2019, in Dundalk, Ireland, denoted as ISA2019, with notes in (Forsyth, van Dam, and Preus 2019) • IEA Wind Task 41 meeting held on January 20, 2020, denoted as IEA41, with comments and recommendations in (IEA Wind 2020a; IEA Wind 2020b) and a comprehensive document being edited (Kelly, Baring-Gould, and Forsyth 2021). General notes: ACP2020 indicated that ACP SWT-1 (ACP 2021) will have a classification based on peak power, with the following ranges: Micro (≤1 kW); Small (1–30 kW), with AM and minimum model validation (power/RPM); Medium (30–65 kW), with AM and more validation, including natural frequency and yaw behavior; Large (65–150 kW), with extensive validation through IEC 61400-13:2015 (load measurements). Table 1 is a proposed revision of the above-mentioned classifications but with a refocusing on rotor-swept area (RSA) as the key classifier. 4 This report is available at no cost from the National Renewable Energy Laboratory at www.nrel.gov/publications. In the following subsections, we identify the key comments and salient points that were extracted from notes and documents associated with the various meetings held. 2.1 Duration Testing A general consensus from DWEA0227 and ISA2019 is that the duration testing (1) is too lengthy and (2) requires wind speeds that are too high. There seems to be uncertainty about what has turned up as problems and failures during the duration tests, and there does not seem to be a clear understanding of what the benefits might be. As mentioned in Forsyth, van Dam, and Preus (2019), a dedicated study should be conducted to assess what the tests have shown so far and what could be done as an alternative to the duration testing, such as component structural testing plus a statistical assessment of operating wind turbines. In Summerville (2020), results and findings for 29 duration tests are presented. The results of that study can be summarized as follows: 1. The duration tests took about 8.8 months to complete, with some taking as long as 14 to 18 months. 2. When failures occurred, they were most often electrical/electronic issues or structural fatigue issues. 3. Of the 29 tests, 22 targeted a Class II rating, with a handful looking at Class III and Class IV. ACP2020 targeted the averaging time associated with the duration testing, as it is a key aspect of the test that contributes to its lengthy nature. Whereas a firm physics basis for the revision of the 5 This report is available at no cost from the National Renewable Energy Laboratory at www.nrel.gov/publications. overall time requirement for the test or the averaging time (1 minute vs. 10 minutes) has not been identified, the SWT-1 committee (NREL 2020b) is proposing to reduce the averaging time from 10 to 1 minute, which is in line with the power performance test requirements for SWTs (IEC 2017). enriched_points.expanded_content: Aerodynamic forces, such as lift and drag, act on turbine blades, influencing their motion and performance, and are crucial in determining system stability under varying wind conditions.
Wind turbine blades are flexible structures that deform under aerodynamic loads, affecting their angle and aerodynamic efficiency, thereby playing a significant role in overall turbine dynamics.
The mass and velocity of turbine blades introduce inertial forces that interact with aerodynamic loads, leading to complex dynamic behaviors essential for predicting turbine performance.
The continuous interaction among aerodynamic forces, elastic deformations, and inertial responses creates feedback loops that can lead to either stability or instability in turbine operations.
Stability analysis is vital for ensuring turbine safety and performance, as it assesses the potential for dynamic instabilities, such as flutter or divergence, that can lead to structural failure.
